speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, John Bond talked about it, about that there was a fault ran from St. Mary's on down along the Potlatch, along the Potlatch Canyon, and that it runs on down to the Salmon River. And I'm not a geologist, so I really don't know all about it. But one day we stood on the rim of the little Potlatch Canyon. They told me to look up the way there, and I could look down and see where the earth sank down, sort of along there, along the little, along the big Potlatch. And he says that there is a fault there that runs from St. Mary's to the Salmon River. Now that would be an interesting thing. I don't think John Miller talked about that in his book, did he?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I don't think so.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Now that'd be an interesting thing to look up about, wouldn't it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, I've heard about those ice caves too. I've heard that there are some, right, between Troy and Kendrick.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, I've heard about that too. Well, it seems like there's this geological fault that runs along there. And I think that might be interesting, because I'm not very good at this earth science, as you know. And I think that's something the kids would be very interested in exploring, about how this fault runs right along through their country. I want to ask you about the play parties. Oh, yes, the play parties. Well, it was customary on Friday evening in the rural schools. The coming of the pioneers, there was very little entertainment. There were no shows, there were no cars, there was no television, there was no radio. So they all had to make their own fun. So each person brought to the community their own ethnic background. The people from different countries brought the singing games of their land. And the people coming here, of course, to this country, they came from other states that had been settled. And each one brought in various forms of pioneer games. And that was one of the chief forms of amusement. It was the rough house of those days. You went down to the schoolhouse, you didn't drink beer, but you played party games. And it was a place for the young men to choose their partners. And this has been going on, of course, since the days of Adam and Eve. So they had all kinds of games they'd play, and most of them were something about choosing partners. And if you weren't allowed to dance at least, they could swing them around a little bit. And that served its purpose. And some of the games they played were, of course, Happy is the Miller Boy who lives by the nail, and the wheel goes round of its own free will, one hand in the hopper and the other in the sack. And we'll all turn together, and we'll all turn back. Everybody reverses, and the boy that's next to the girl grabs him a partner. And another one went like this. We're sailing east, we're sailing west, we're sailing over the ocean. And say, young man, if you want to get a wife, you've got to be quick in the motion. And they grabbed the girl nearest them, and away they went. And of course there was Skip to My Lou, my darling, with all its various verses. They must be, oh, 46 verses to it all together. And everybody that came from a different section of the country could add a few verses as it was sung in their country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was Skip to My Lou played?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Do you remember how they... Yes, they all got in a big circle. And you chose partners, and you stood and held hands in this circle. And they sang about, my wife wears a number ten shoe, my wife wears a number ten shoe, my wife wears a number ten shoe, Skip to My Lou, my darling. And then you took a hold of your girlfriend, and you swung her in a circle round and round and round, you know. And then they'd get in a big circle again. And then when it came to, we'll all turn together and we'll all turn back. One stepped ahead, the boy would step ahead, and the girl would fall back, and that gave you each a new partner. And they played lots of other choosing partner games. That was the best. And it was a chance for the young people to meet together and have fun under the very watchful eyes and chaperone of the old folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, they did this without music?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: They sang. And they all mostly sang. Of course, in some of the very early, even the earliest people brought some forms of music like violins and some school houses, but the first thing they would buy would be a little weed organ. And with someone pumping on the organ, you got a little rhythm and a little beat to it, you know. But if they didn't have anything, they could still sing and clap, you know, clap in rhythm. And that was all they needed. When young people get together, you know, they can entertain themselves without much artificial music and bass. And they played guitars, even in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, yes. Lots of them had guitars. And they would play guitars and sang. But I thought, when they call it play parties, this is to mean that it isn't a dance. Oh, well, no, it was these play party games were, there were other games, too, that they played. They would play games like Splittin' the Platter, and they'd get out and they'd twirl a pipe in around in the floor, and then they'd call out the name of some girl and she'd run and see if she could catch it. And if she caught it, maybe she would get a prize, or if she didn't catch it. And she had to be the twirler for the next game. And they played charades. And everybody would act out something, you know, and you acted out different games. And they would play, and they played games like, and if you could guess it, that'd be fun. And if not, you had to pay a forfeit. And some of the forfeits, you know, would be like kissing games. Some very pretty girl, she would be in great demand, and she'd have to give a forfeit of kissing some other boys there. And that caused great hilarity and great fun. That was okay. That was okay, yes, that was just fine, even in those days. So you see, they had a little something going on in those days. But I thought that in some places, they played these play party games instead of dancing. They didn't approve of the regular kind of dance. Yes, well, that was right. Suddenly, you could always get by playing, and Skip to My Lou, and London Bridge is falling down.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And happy is the Miller boy. If they wouldn't allow you to dance, at least you could play these games, and then you got to swing your partners. And having a girl in your arms tonight was just about the same whether you were dancing Black Bottoms or if you were playing Miller Boy. Black Bottoms. That was some kind of a dance they used to dance back many years ago. Something like that. What do they call them now? What other dances the kids do now? It isn't what to see anymore, is it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No, and it's not Charleston. That was in my age. I belonged to the Flapper age, and everybody was out doing the Charleston, you know. But now, do they do the Big Apple?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No, that's out of style, too. No, they just stand there and move around. They just stand there and make motions. Make suggestive motions. Let's put it that way. They just stand there and go through suggestive motions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, this was usually associated with the schoolhouse?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, yes. Of course, they did meet in each other's homes, too. But the schoolhouse had more room, and you had more freedom. The schoolhouse generally had little better lights than they had in their own private homes. Can the light in one little kerosene lamp went very conducive to big crowds. But the schoolhouse often had a gasoline lantern or something like that to light it up. And the young people then, I like the young people now, they didn't need too much light.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: They enjoyed it anyway. And who are we to say who had the most fun, you know? You think they had lots of fun in those?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, yes. Yes, I have a bunch of letters from folks I talked 50 years ago. And every one of them was telling me how much they missed the good old times they had in the good old days. The good parties that they used to have and the schoolhouses were in use every day of the week. Saturday night, they had their big play parties, and on Sunday, they had the Sunday school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This would be, say, every Saturday night?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, just practically, yes. Practically, they'd have surprise parties in each other's houses, too. If the schools got too busy with their scheduled things, then people would open their homes, and the young folks and the old folks and the little folks would go to each other's houses and have a party.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would these sometimes last all night?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, I think so, yes. I can remember getting drifted in in one of my early school teaching days. I remember the snowmen drifting so badly, we couldn't get home if we wanted to, so we just stayed all night and then went home in the morning. You know the song about, and we danced all night to broad daylight, didn't go home with the girls in the morning. So they had little all-night parties in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it usually during the winter?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Usually in the winter, because in the summer, everybody was so concerned with putting in crops and harvesting the crops and that sort of thing. They didn't have time for much sociability.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this made it a way of getting together, passing the time?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: That's right. For the long winter evenings, it broke the monotony.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, were there other things that would be a part of the program?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, yes. Some, yes. Sometimes the schools would have what they called a basket supper, and all the girls made pretty baskets and brought them. And it was a sort of a charity thing. If they needed some new records, or if they needed a Victrola, if they needed even just some new lamps, they would have a benefit. And the girls would bring the suppers, and the boys would have to bid on them on the baskets to see who would get to eat with the girl of their choice. And the money would go to the school. And they had pie suppers. Every girl would bake a pie and bring them much spirited bidding among the married men, as well as the single men, and go to the man that didn't buy his own wife's pie. I'll tell you, that was bad. That was bad.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And those were some of the other things. Well, now, let's say that they were going to have a play party night at school. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they have things that going on that night at the school?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, yes. Often the school children would start out with their program. They'd have like a little Thanksgiving program, maybe. And each would have a little recitation, maybe a little play about the coming of the pilgrims and about why we should be thankful. And when that was over, then the other folks would shove back the chairs and maybe play singing games, as they called them, or maybe play some play party games. And they'd have lunch, and then they'd go home. And that was the social life of the rural schools.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were singing games the same as by far?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, the singing games were. Well, they were the ones that you sang to. And play party games could be anything like the Spin the Platter and Post Office and musical chairs or Cross Christians and Silly Answers and all those kind of games. They, for you didn't sing, the guessing games and that sort of thing, or the singing games. Well, that could be play party games. Oh, yes, yes. Lots of people call play party games. They think of it only in terms of singing games, but that's not so. There were lots of other party games where you didn't necessarily sing, like musical chairs and like Spin the Platter forfeits, anything where you had to guess something and get it wrong. And that was the paying of a forfeit for being wrong. Where do these plays come from, that the kids would- Sometimes they would make them up, and those were the best of all if they had a little history that they were interested in and could write a little pilgrim play. And each of some people could be the Indians and some pilgrims and others, something else, you know, and if they wrote their own and what they thought they said, that would show a lot of imagination and creativity. And they learned to spell incidentally and use proper grammar, and then they dramatized it and put it on for the benefit of their folks. Sometimes they just learned pieces that they learned regularly in school. Generally, there were little verses that had a good moral teaching to them, you know, and often they learned pieces like Little Boy Blue and so on from their literature books. And then they would, whoever got to know them the best and could speak them the best, they would have them- they would get to elocute them for their parents, for the- and join their parents at the program, the school program.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ever put on melodramas?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh yes, yes. Sometimes the young people of the community, if they were ambitious and felt the need of getting together, why they would have plays. And there was a publishing company called the Sam French Company, and they turned out regular line of plays to be put on by rural schools. It didn't take much scenery, it didn't take much costuming, and generally they were funny ones, you know, that would appeal to the audience and send you home laughing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the feed? They have big, big feed when you have a play party night?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, and they often had that. Yes, sometimes- sometimes they generally sandwiches and cookies and maybe cake and coffee, generally always coffee. And that would be sometimes the communities would get together maybe for New Year's Day. Christmas Day they didn't very often get together, but Thanksgiving and New Year's, many of the communities made a regular habit of putting on a big Thanksgiving dinner at the schoolhouse and everybody come. And New Year's was another favorite time for them all to get together and eat a big New Year's dinner and then dance in the evening and in the afternoon and evening and visit. And those were good things to do with roads drifted and snow blowing, and people were very limited in how far they could go for a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was it that decided if there was going to be a night party? Would it be the teacher?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I think generally it was the teacher that promoted it. That was part of their all-around bit of public relations, would be to say that the young folks had a chance to get together and visit. And she was done the official chaperone. She and the school board would give official chaperones for these parties and things. And that was kind of the way it worked out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they called the evening? An entertainment or a party?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, school entertainment. Sometimes they called it the school program. And sometimes it was just we're going down to the school for a little party. And sometimes there were women's clubs. Later on, there got to be women's clubs in the communities. And then the women's clubs were great at sponsoring some kind of a doings and entertaining their husbands and their friends and the young people. But in the very oldest times, in the very earliest beginnings, I think it was largely sponsored by the teachers. Another thing they did almost before, before I was a teacher in the community, was having taffy poles. And that was another chance for young people to get together and socialize. And it didn't matter if they were pulling candy or what they were doing. It was an opportunity to meet and get acquainted. So they served a real purpose, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people of all ages dance in the play party games?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, they were. Well, it was mostly the young people. But it just depended on the community. And some of the communities, the old people came too. And they'd get out. Especially for the dance square dances. Like Virginia Reel and all the old swing them on the corner like you're swinging them on the gate. All the old dances, they were callers to be a caller. And then they generally had a violin and an organ, the school organ. And they'd have somebody who could play the fiddle. And they'd dance. And they did that from the beginning, I think, of Homestead Days. Because someone wrote a very lovely poem about the boy playing the fiddle. So she could dance on the punchy on floor. And a punch on floor, of course, is a sod floor. Where the sod's just been turned up. So that would be kind of hard dancing. But I guess they did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did they continue to have the school entertainments?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, until the schools were, yes, until the schools were consolidated. They did it up to the very last. And that's the one thing the rural people missed more than anything else. Was it took away the center of their social life. The life of the community had centered around the schools. The dancing. The square dancing. The play party games. The entertainments marking the seasons. The interest in what their children were doing. So all those things entered into it. Now here comes my... Now turn it off a minute. Here comes my sister and Tom. Well, sure. You can't do that for a couple of days. Oh, it doesn't matter. It doesn't make any sense. Record, sure. That's better put down that way because then you get it the way you say it. Uh-huh. Well, you would form a circle. Everybody holding hands. And then you would start and sing. Happy is the Miller boy who lives by the meal. The wheel goes round of its own free will. One hand in the hopper and the other in the sack. The lady stepped forward and the gents fall back. And that way you would change partners. And when you go around the circle again, say the same thing. Happy is the Miller boy who lives by the meal. The wheel goes round of its own free will. One hand in the hopper and the other in the sack. The lady stepped forward and the gents fall back. Then you change partners. But in the meantime, there'd be somebody who was it who would be in the middle. And he would jump in and try to grab a partner. Sold it. The one who lost his partner would have to come into the center. Then sometimes the call or the singers would change it like a lady stepped forward. You see, or sometimes a gent would step forward. Sometimes a lady would step forward. And that would give you a different partner. That was happy as a Miller boy.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Let's see. What else did we have?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, skip to the blue. You have that here already. I think you had partners for that. And you stood in a circle, but each had a partner. And you would and then somebody would be it that's in the middle. And that would be skip, skip, skip to the blue. Skip, skip, skip to the blue. Skip, skip, skip to the blue. Skip to the blue, my darling. And one girl in the skipper, she'd get out and skip around. And she stopped in front of the boyfriend. Oh, and she chose him. And then they swing. And then everybody's swaying. Uh-huh. Is that right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I think so.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And so then gone again, what will I do? Gone again, what will I do? Gone again, what will I do?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Skip to the blue, my darling. That would be if they lost their partner, you see. And they had many, many verses. They'd make up verses too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now they lose your partner?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, somebody would come out from the center. You'd have this it person in the center who had no partner. And they would come out and get your partner from you. And then you would be at the end. And you have to get on and dance with somebody else. A similar one to that would be a pig in the parlor. And everybody formed in a circle, excepting one who has chosen to be the pig. And the pig had to stand in the middle of this circle. And then he sang, uh, uh, my father and mother were Irish. And I was Irish too. We bought a wee piggy for six pence and it was Irish too. We kept the pig in the parlor. We kept the pig in the parlor. We kept the pig in the parlor. And it was Irish too. Now at a certain spot there, you all changed partners. And, or did you do a grand right and left?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes. That's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's a grand right and left?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Get up here, dad. And we'll show them what a grand right and left is. Same thing as it is in square dances. Uh-huh. You stand the circle. Get up. Uh-huh. Let's go. And then roll. We'll get the girls. Your right hand too. Well, now your right hand to your right. Your right hand to your right. Your left hand to your neighbor. Yeah. Your right hand to your right. Roll it right. Yeah. Your left hand to your neighbor. And my right hand to the next unit. And we all promenade. Oh, we were. Yeah. Yeah, I see.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And we have to change the model. We were, we did it a little wrong, because the girls were always, we were standing wrong. Oh, yeah. Yeah. The girls would go this way. I did it. You would go that way. Oh, yeah. With your hand next. Oh, yes. Uh-huh. With your right hand. Dear partner, you take your right hand. And you would go inside of her. I would go behind you and take her. You call that a grand right and left hand. She's my neighbor. The one with your right hand. And for anybody who gets mixed up, then you sing it. And you just sing to them your right hand to your partner. Your left hand to your neighbor. The third one is your partner. And we'll all promenade. That's when there's four and aha. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah. This would be singing. No, no instruments. Oh, could be both. But usually just singing. Yes. Now, this man who led it in our community, he just sang with a good loud voice. And we all followed after everybody tried to sing. Of course, a lot of square dances were sung, too. Yes. A lot of square dances were sung.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they figured that this was like this kind of, these ones that we're just talking about, would these be dances or would these be games?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: We call them games. And if not, you'll find, was this way, in the country communities, some objected to dancing. But if you called them games, you got by with them, you see. But others didn't care. And then you had the square dances. There's really very little difference. A game usually had an it. And the dances, usually just changing a partner, just granted for partner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember, one that I wrote down looked interesting to me, was water, water, wine, flour? Do you ever hear that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No. No, that's a strange one.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, are you researching games? Is that what you're interested in?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, we're talking about the old times in the county. In general. In the museum in general, yeah. But this is something that I know Lola knows about. The one that she's been talking about. The one that she's been talking about would be the hills. Yeah, not around. That wildflower, wildflower growing up so high, something sorry, surely she must die. But they sent someone to see her and she revived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well, what will they do for her?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Wildflower, wildflower growing up so high, who's so sorry, so sorry, a little so and so must die. What shall we do for her?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: We will send Sam Schrager to see her, see her, see her. We'll send Sam Schrager to see her and she'll be all right now. That would be more that the kids would play, because I never saw that played in the games at the schoolhouse where the adults were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the kids and adults both play or would just be the adults who played the car and kids?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Both would get out and play at the party game. Largely the young adults, but anybody would come and play. And lots of times they'd take a young adult, he would see some oldish lady and he'd run and get her and bring her out and she'd have fun. Lots of times the little kids rock. Well, they did pretty well mixed up. Well, the doors came as well as the... Some could be played outdoors. There was a grandpa and her chief of car. Oh yeah, this one that Lola names is, the little children would often play that one outdoors about wildflower, wildflower. And now, that's the first time I ever heard that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's dropped the handkerchief?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh boy, that's an outside game. Well, you can play it inside too, we did. Yes, you could. Especially if you had to get a piece in. You all get together in a circle, a ring. And there is an it. Yeah, this is an it. And it comes around the outside with a handkerchief. It drops it behind somebody and somebody is supposed to chase them and catch them.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: What happens?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, it goes like this. If they get tear around the circle before she gets caught, then the guy that has the handkerchief or that was dropped behind is it. But if he catches her before, then he goes back in his own space and she has to be it. She has to be it. Uh-huh. And. A simple game. But that's an outdoors game mostly for kids. It dropped the handkerchief for kids. I never saw adults joining that much, very much at a time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would party games usually be at the schoolhouse?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, they would always be at the house. Then there's one called Post Office. Tell them about Post Office. You do that, Tommy. You do it. I never played. You get a kick out of everything. I never did know. I never played Post Office. Oh, sir, I never did know. I've heard that. They didn't believe in kissing in my neighborhood.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: That came in to Post Office. They questioned Abby about it and she couldn't give them power. Well, if you had to answer, you went and made a letter. You went to go and made a letter. And one stamp, it took one stamp, that was one kiss. And if it was something else, I took two stamps. How many stamps does this one take?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: You got special deliveries.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: How did you get to match?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yeah, special deliveries.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, you have to go in and see the Postmaster?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yeah, somebody was Postmaster, yeah.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Were they in a darkened place?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I don't know. I never end up. My schools wouldn't stand for that kind of stuff. No, they wouldn't mind either. No, my school wouldn't. They left out the best part.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, I'll tell you, the way I saw the kids play it, did you get a letter?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And I did.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Did you stamp it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And they'd stand right on your foot. That's all it was for that part. Yeah, but what's the... This I don't know about this other. Then there was, in all kinds of games, there was a thing of redeeming forfeits. You heard about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, I don't know. In some game or other, if you lose, you have to put up some article, pocket knife, watch, pencil, whatever you had, for having lost, I guess. Then when everybody had lost something, then you got to redeem your forfeit. And you got up there. Now then, I'll be... You can hold it over my head now. Tell me part of it. Heavy, heavy. Heavy, heavy, heavy. What shall the owner do to redeem it? What must the owner of this pretty thing do to redeem it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, pretty thing.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Fine or super fine?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: If it's a man, if it's a boy, he says fine. If it's a girl, it always says super fine. It's fine.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl

And often one older boy or girl who is a fast runner, when they go on the schoolground, instead of having any argument or counting out or anything, just say, I'll be it. And volunteer and get out there. Then the kids are. It took all noon hour to get the it decided. Right. You know, trying to go back. It sure took all the recesses. Well when I was young the most popular game like that was capturing the flag. That was a similar game. Where you had two prisons and each side had a flag that was guarded by the side. Yes. And there were two territories. And since you crossed into the other side's territory they could they hit you three times you had to go to prison. Ah fine. And the object was for one person to steal a flag. They managed to get that flag and get it back over on his side. Same thing. You hit the people who were in the prison and then they were free. Yes. Now this that's what we called steel sticks. And instead of having a flag we just had sticks. And you try to steal each other's sticks. But that was just like the other things. Our country schools would have only one big flag and that would have to be raised on the flagpole. Oh boy. You treated it with respect. But you could gather up quite a few little sticks around the woodshed and steal each other sticks. That was pretty good too. Well it seems like these play parties that they didn't you don't hear about them much. You hear about the entertainment you know that there'd be entertainments. A good party. First of all would come your little program where the children perform then would come refreshments. And if they didn't allow that you would have these games which are practically the same as a square dance. And those we said would be like skip to Maloo and happy as a mellow boy. Like them by the end of the story and we ran all night. So we never saw any bubbles. But you saw lots of bosoms because all these little kids had to be fed. And you didn't go out and 20 below zero weather defeated. No. Ladies kind of demurely sat over in the lady's side of the room more or less. Very graciously and gently sort of stuck the little babies in the underneath the blouse or something and nourished it. Well at a Mother's Day school program and this will slay you. It was a good morning community where I was teaching. And we invited the mothers into tea. Well in the little tea time, the little babies were getting hungry. Some mothers just sat there openly and unashamedly and proud. They were just their babies. And some of these elderly babies that were critic teachers there, oh they looked down their noses. How rude and how vulgar. I said gals, this is Mother's Day. These mothers were just exercising the first privilege of motherhood. And I said I'm for it. Those babies needed refreshments far more than these big fat old teachers sitting around. They're looking down their noses. And that was right. Another interesting thing about it was to the kids. Most of the great school kids it wasn't worth looking at. And as soon as you thought that it was worth looking at, well then you'd been so well indoctrinated that you were afraid. And you didn't know what direction to look. Your face turned red if you even opened your eyes.

And you couldn't really call it an anch. You called it a game. But it was Virginia Rio.

words: You did run through, Skip to My Lou, didn't you?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes. Oh, yeah, you said that, yeah. It's funny. It just seems like without music, I'm so accustomed to thinking of having music with any kind of dance that I think of. The idea of being able to do it without music, it must have been- Well, you know, I'll say it. You know, the work was simple, and you all knew them. And this man in our community had this big voice, a splendid singer. He could, oh, he could just sing anything. And everybody followed after him, and it was lots of music there without instrumental music. But a bit. Rise in the sugar bowl two by two. Rise in the sugar bowl two by two. You got it. Yeah. Rise in the sugar bowl two by two. Skip to the moon, my darling. You got it, darling. That's about all there was to it. That's right. I like that. Gone again. What do I do?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I don't remember how you played it, though, but the partner was gone. Yeah, yeah, you just get it. There was one extra one always.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And you came in this one with this one, see? And then that left that other one out, see?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: That was the one that we stood by the partners, in a big circle, partners. And then when the one that was in the circle came up and took your partner, then you had to get out in the circle and go and swing somebody. Then that left that girl, and sometimes it'd run all the way around the circle and then pick somebody, and then that left an extra person. Just that one. A way of mixing. Oh, yeah, a mixer. What you'd call, you know, dance as they call them, a mixer. Oh, this would be a mixer. Yeah. The one in the middle would come and choose a different partner. Well, I was wondering about the narrative customs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they differences the way it was then and now?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I'm sure the courting was different because the attitudes about it were different. Well, for one thing, a horse didn't run off the road when you were in a buggy. He didn't wait at home. He didn't have to park. Well, now, I can tell you a wedding that took place when I was in the great schools. The oldest daughter in the family, an older sister to this man was such a good singer, could keep the program going. She did, I guess, about 22. And the young man from the community, and he was about 23. And one day, the children of my age didn't appear at school. And somebody said, Martha is getting married today. And so as we came by the home of the bride that night, the Hill House, we looked up there. And here we saw all the kids that we were in school with all dressed up. Everyone was dressed up. And the two boys had on their knee pants. That's their best suits. You didn't wear long pants. The pants came to the knees. And we knew that the wedding had been right there that day. But just the family wedding it was. And right in the home, and the minister from the Lutheran Church had come out. Well, this young woman, her uncle was a minister. But whether he was in Moscow at that time or not, I won't know. But anyway, the wedding was a home wedding, many more home weddings, and not much church weddings. That's right. It's mostly home weddings. Home weddings. Church weddings were expensive. We could stand a little bit of description about me. Well, Jim Cameron like to describe how he looked. He said, I always had a big stomach. And they always had knee pants. I always had knee pants. And he said, I look just like a toad. I always looked just like a toad. And you look at Jim as he told this, you could kind of believe it. He was convincing. But these were quite a style. There was a little button and a tab on the bottom of these pants. And you're either supposed to have that button above your knee or below your knee. And nobody ever was sure. So usually you had one above one knee and one below the other knee. And then, of course, you couldn't have your bare legs down underneath. So you had long stockings that pinned up onto something up above. Now, some of those things were called panty waste. And they had little garters. That's where panty waste came from. That's where panty. Yeah. And some of these panty waste had a little kind of a rubber biscuit on the end of a thing with a sort of a bitch link. Slipped up over the biscuit. You stuck the biscuit up through the stocking. And you snicked this up over it there. And that was supposed to suffice. There are all kinds of ingenious devices for fastening up your stocking. Now, one way was a safety pin that fastened all to your long woolen underwear up here some place. And the other end had one of those biscuits and bitch links on the lower end. But, of course, usually something went to hell with the biscuit. And so you ended up by pinning the safety pin through the stocking and through your long part of your underwear. That left the biscuit with a bitch link hanging down out of the bottom end of your knickers or your whatever you call them. And more often than not, if you played these games with any enthusiasm at all, something happened to the stockings. Usually just one leg of the two, you know?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And the stocking would dangle down around the top of your shoe. Of course, if you had high shoes, you didn't handicap too much. But if the biscuit was still hanging on and the safety pin came loose, why, that kind of made a tail. And if people stepped on that, would your play drop the handkerchief?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, that slowed you down momentarily. That'd be no good. Well, in spite of that, that was the only real bad thing that happened to boys in my youth was that when you were dressed up, you had to wear these neat pants. Otherwise, you could wear bed by overalls and have fun. But you went there to parties. You were supposed to get dressed up in these things. You were supposed to. Although in the country schools, kids got to wear overalls quite a bit. The mothers even got tired of trying to keep these things prepared in working order. Except Mrs. Brazier. She just had one little child. She just had one kid. She had to wear stuff that I wouldn't have been caught dead with. You wouldn't be caught dead, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this an Eastern style, you think?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No, this all Western. I don't know where it started. It was the only kind of pants you could buy. You couldn't, boys couldn't have long pants till they were 16. Now, I don't know why that was. And if it was a real building mother, she might wait till he was 17. Howard Schuler got his first pair of long pants, I think, when you were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear of him?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I didn't talk to you last summer. No, by gosh. He had to borrow mine. His mother wouldn't let him. And I got mine because my cousin was older and I wasn't married. That was a practical, that was a practical, sensible way to do it. And Howard got to wear mine because he was in a play. And I was sure mad at him too, because I wanted to wear it. It was the only pair of long pants in the neighborhood. The neighborhood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When would people get married? What's about what age?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, when the girl was about 16, the man was about 30. That was earlier. That was earlier that they married about that, those ages. But this couple that I remember wouldn't choose. Mark was about 22 and wasn't Carl about 23. Not more than 25. And that's the only wedding that happened around there. The girls wanted to get married because that was probably for people who didn't have any talent. The girls were also thinking about a career. Now, this is a little bit more of that too. When you were young, when you were doing that. Well, I knew my high school, my high school. Yeah, high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That would have been when? It would be in the 20s?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: That was one girl got married, Mary Loyler, Meryl Loyler. And everybody thought, oh gosh, isn't that awful?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: She's such a smart girl. Isn't that awful?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, I can't be worse one than that. Somebody came home and told me over Canfield that they heard one of the girls was engaged. Well, she said engaged. I didn't even know she was pregnant yet. Of course, that was more of the recent days. That was more of the recent days. And when I was in high school, there was a girl who didn't come back to school the next year. And there was much pronounce. She committed suicide. Nobody ever said why, but everybody kind of wondered. It was that serious. Yeah, that serious. Oh, Aaron Cuthorne. Yeah, that was bad. But I was thinking, though, of the time that like when I was small in the grades. Now, that's the only wedding I can think of was like, I remember the Jones got married and they came out in a buggy and the minister came later and the minister lost his way and he went over to Mrs. Anderson's place way down in there. And Mr. Anderson's without cleaning up the barn and he stuck his head out through the manure hole and the minister said, is this the home where they're having the wedding today?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: He said, no, he says we've had too many weddings here already. And he's banging the manure.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: He had one. Well, what usually happened, the girl, well, some of them got married young, but usually the girl, if she was going out of school, she went and taught school. She got a job or she worked in somebody's house for a while and didn't get married until later. Well, this young lady had graduated from the eighth grade and lots of times they didn't go on to high school at all. Eighth grade is real good if you got through that. And then she had done, as you say, gone in and worked in town for Dr. Von End, I guess, is, you know, Fonanda or, well, anyway, that's the one. She went in and worked in his home. Yeah, that's the man. She worked in his home, I think, or some home in town, anyway. And then, when she got up to a suitable age, she married this neighbor boy she'd been going with. They'd been going to church together, too. They were illusory. I must say, I'm surprised to hear you say that young women were thinking careers when you were in high school. Yes, that's the end of the thirties. Well, she taught school years before she got married, so did Lola. Well, years after she was married. Yes, then I was going to say about, oh, Mary Alice. Mary Alice was a young woman in high school at Tom. And she said, when a girl, she graduated in 1930, huh?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: In 1929, yeah. 1929, she graduated from the 90s and liked so many. She went to normal school long enough to get married. And she said that they'd say, when there was a group of them together, say, so-and-so, namely, so-and-so, she's getting married. She's getting married. Doesn't she have any talent?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: See, this is all she would do. There were a few girls that were set on marriage. Yeah, not very many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think this idea came in?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Because nowadays, we tend to think of it as sort of a idea. But I know it isn't, from what you're saying. You know, the idea of a woman having a career of her own. And it's not her career because she wants things. Then she had to have a career because she wanted to eat. Yes. And also, she wanted money. Now, as she was standard of living, then it was almost survival. You had to get out of the family home pretty quick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But most of the mothers of the girls hadn't been teachers too, had they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Most of them had done something. Well, another thing, too. A lot of these mothers had so many children and such an awful, hard life of cooking and raising these children. And they just hated to see their girls go into something they thought would be like this. And they thought it would be so much better if the girls would have a career. If you got married and had a kid, you were trapped for the rest of your life. That's the way the mothers taught their daughters. Yeah. Yeah. And there was some justification, too. Well, of course, these were mostly children of the people who were more or less pioneers. Right. Well, it was the children of the generation after the pioneers. Like, your grandfather would have been the original pioneer, then your father. And people of that age would be speaking against marriage. Right. To this country in 1879, just as an example, and it's quite typical of everyone. He had 13 kids.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And times got so hard that even with the free land and all, it was just the problem of clothing and feeding those kids. And then by the time those kids were raised, or some maybe just a little older, like, say, my mother, for example, she sure thought that this was awfully foolish to, for a woman, as she said, to make a brutesale of herself and to have all these children, which you couldn't provide for yourself or your kids. And you can go up to the cemetery in Moscow here right now. You'll see it's not hard to find a family name. Baby after baby after baby after baby. There is one year. Yeah, there's one good one.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And so you can see why these mothers then taught their daughters, have a career, don't get married, make your own living, make your own way. At least until you have a start. Now, my dad, just for the example, the difference between us, my generation, most kids didn't even think of going on to college. A lot of them didn't finish high school, and they didn't have to either. But my dad, he thought very highly of an education because he saw how handicapped he was without it. He'd only gone to the fourth grade, I think. And at one time later, he got, at his own expense, got to take some high school courses in the wintertime. And with me now, I went to college for three years and then had to quit. And Donald, he didn't even, that's the brother of this Mary Alice, he didn't get to go to college. He went to normal school and got a teacher certificate, which you could get in two years. And so did his sister, Mary Alice. And so did you girls. You got your teacher certificate first and then went to college later at your own expense. And so many people did. Many of the guys that I went to college with were doing it the same way. They were older than I was. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how much opportunity was really open to women as far as the different choices they could make?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: It seems like most of them became teachers. Well, they could do this. They could teach, or they could be librarians, or they could, well, this would be teaching too, but give music lessons, teach that. They could be a seamstress. They could make hats. They would work in a millinery store. And of course, all of you dreamed of going to Hollywood and being in the movies. All of the, they say, all of the it girls. We could have all been parables. They hadn't given that for George and the children. That was pretty strong. And people were really influenced by the movies. Oh, it was the only thing. It was, it was fairyland. It was the thing, wasn't it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I don't know of anything now that corresponds to what the program was. Well, it was the people who were going to English teachers. And we'd have been good ones. No, I was going to be a writer. Oh. But I never got a chance to be that, because just about the time I'd sit down, write one sentence, then I'd have to go out and chase the pigs, or cook some food, or something.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: But that was in my mind that I would make my living writing short stories. And if she never got started to that, she would never have raised her one check or had it even. That's right. That's right. Yeah. That was my idea. When you get right down to it, though, that there weren't that many different possibilities that women could do in those, not compared to the way it is now. Teaching was the main out if you were a smart, intelligent woman. And marriage was about what was left for the stragglers. Well, sewing, though, you know. Yeah. Some of them had talent. And, of course, there's a lot of people

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: attracted by the entertainment field. There's a lot of modeling. The girls that have the whatever it takes. And running little shops, ready to rehearse. Yeah. That was done. Yes, clerking, too. Clerking. Yeah. In a store. Well, I was going to be a teacher who I went down to Alden or once on. These old women, 75 years old, still teaching. And everybody was saying about them, when are they going to die? Aren't they ever going to die?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: The kids would say to me, you know. And teaching. Oh, I better hurry home before Dad or Earl Clark changes his mind. About two generations earlier, teaching was a man's name, man's field. Yeah. They were the, that was the pioneer generation. Yeah. It was a school master then. But in my time, school teaching was a woman's job. It's almost reserved for women. You're almost the main, what would you call it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Social season. The social season. It always occurred when farm, farm market was not going too strongly. Usually, in the fall of the year after harvest, when everybody had little money. But it did occur other times a year, too. Farm market. This still happened. It's still the same thing. When you got out of business, that's the way you did it. Change farther, even when you wanted to get rid of surplus stuff. Yeah. You know, you had, yes, my dad had sale. I had one too. Just to get rid of stuff that was obsolete as far as you were concerned. But it turned out to be a real important part of social life then, too, to be able to get together. Well, you saw all your neighbors and got acquainted with people from further away. Yeah, that was part of it. In those days, of course, these sales all furnished a free lunch at noon. But since the Depression, too many people came for the free lunch and crowded out the buyers. But all the ladies are going to sit in each other's cars and the old days and the bunnies and they'd sit and visit each other and got acquainted. I think it was quite the social season all the way. Yeah. I sure loved them as a kid. And you got a few miles away from home, even if you just went over the hill. You'd never get over the hill. You didn't know what was over there. And that was great stuff to get the idea to go over. For example, we went to a sale in the Tammany country, I guess. Now, that Tammany is the south of Lewiston. Or at least it was in the Lewiston Orchards. And we went from our farm near Genesee. That's 20 miles from Lewiston. We went down the day before. We went out to the sale and then we came home either that night or the next day. That was by team, of course, you see.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Have you been to some of the auction sales around? Just the same. Just the same thing.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Just the same. Just the same thing. Except that there was always the stock, the livestock and the cattle. Oh, it's pretty. It's just like being to a stock show. We're always quite on track. That was your mobile power for farming. So, lots and lots of time there. They always kept the horses till the last because that would hold the crowd. Everybody came to either see how horses sold or to buy an extra horse or to buy a good horse or to get a mate for one that he had. Some such thing. And harness. Gus still can't go to a sale without bidding on harness.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: You grew up there by Genesee then. Yeah. So, you probably have strong memories of the stock show. Yeah. Of course, Gus would remember that better than I was. But I was about all in the greats then when the stock shows were going. That was long about, well, World War I before World War I. They had lovely stock shows more recently in Uniontown, though. But these were the big things out there where the mill now is. Where the pond is. Where they've got there. Oh, the Lewiston. Yeah, Lewiston stock show. That was the big deal. That's where the Plat boys always took their cattle. I was thinking of the Genesee stock show. Well, I remember them too. They were a little bit overshadowed when they had the Lewiston ones, so. Yeah, we heard Bob Gray, you know, telling me the other day about how he punished off for the greasy ... horses and brought them into the stock show. I was pretty young when they had the Genesee stock shows. That was when Mildred was, before I went to school. So, my memories are not too good. Five. I think we were about five years old. Uniontown had then the stock shows just up until, oh, 20 years ago. Yeah. Since we were in Pomeroy. I tried a good chair to sit here because.
